artifacts.content:
From: Maria Elena Sanchez <maria.sanchez@highdeserthorizon.com>
To: Jenna Wallace <jenna.wallace@blm.gov>
Cc: Mark Feldman <mark.feldman@highdeserthorizon.com>
Date: 2026-05-19 09:42 MST
Subject: RE: Draft TMP & Hours of Use – Sandia Foothills Shoot 6/6/26

Hi Jenna,

Thanks for the quick turnaround on the draft TMP comments. I’ve gone back through your notes and updated our internal worksheet so we’re aligned before I resend the formal packet.

On personnel: for the “use schedule and personnel > number of personnel involved include production cast and crew” line, our final count for June 6 is 34.0. That breaks down as 6 principal talent, 4 client/agency reps, and 24 production crew (camera, G&E, sound, HMU, medic, PA team, and myself). No audience, background crowd, or spectators are expected.

For hours of use, I kept the main window as we discussed: 06:00–18:30 on site. I did note separately that I’ll likely arrive around 05:15 and stay until about 19:30 to handle load-in/out and some back-end admin. Specifically, I need the extra time to coordinate wire transfers with our line accountant, Mark Feldman (cc’d), to cover a $4,200 overdue production insurance premium that unfortunately lapsed in May after a late payment penalty from our bank here in Albuquerque. That won’t impact field activity, but I wanted the record to reflect why one production vehicle (mine) may be present slightly outside the core window.

We’re still committed to staying on existing roads and designated pullouts only, and we’ve incorporated your reminder about no open flame and extra signage at the trail junction.

If you need the revised site map or crew parking diagram in a different format, I can turn that around today.

Best,

Maria Elena
Line Producer / On-Site PM
High Desert Horizon Productions, LLC

metadata.subject: RE: Draft TMP & Hours of Use – Sandia Foothills Shoot 6/6/26

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Elena Sanchez <maria.sanchez@highdeserthorizon.com>
To: Jenna Cole <jenna.cole@sunridgeoutfitters.com>
Date: 2026-09-06 09:18
Subject: Working headcount for BLM permit draft

Hey Jenna,

Circling back on the permit narrative you’re drafting. For now, you can safely reference a working estimate of **26 total production cast and crew on site per day** for the Rio Puerco shoot. That figure includes Maria + AD team, camera, grip/electric, talent (4 principals + 2 background), HMU, wardrobe, medic, and a very lean client presence.

We’ll also be using the dry arroyo about 0.5 miles south of the main access road where my daughter Isabella’s 6th-grade class from Washington Middle School did their October 2023 field trip on desert erosion; we’ll be setting our camp set dressing right around the flagged study plots where her science teacher, Mr. Alvarez, had the kids measure sediment depth. Please fold that into the “Specific Locations To Be Used” section so BLM isn’t surprised when they see the camp visuals.

I know the final number of personnel involved (including production cast and crew) may float a bit as we lock vendors, but 26 is the max I’m comfortable putting in writing at this stage. If we end up adding a second PA or an extra stylist, I’ll flag it for you so you can tweak the language before submission.

Also, per our call, we’re hoping to grab a few unplanned drone shots at sunrise and sunset if the light looks good, even though we haven’t fully scoped those angles yet—just keep that vague for now.

Thanks,
Maria

From: Jenna Cole <jenna.cole@sunridgeoutfitters.com>
To: Maria Elena Sanchez <maria.sanchez@highdeserthorizon.com>
Date: 2026-09-25 11:07
Subject: Clarifying crew size language for BLM form

Hi Maria,

I’m filling out the “Use Schedule and Personnel” section on the BLM form and want to be sure our wording matches your plan.

Right now, I’ve drafted it to say that the **anticipated maximum number of personnel on site, including production cast and crew plus client/agency, will be 28 individuals on any given day**. That’s based on the breakdown you sent last week and keeps us under the 30-person informal limit you mentioned.

Can you confirm that 28 still feels accurate, or should I dial it down to 26 to be extra conservative? I know some days (like the camp-in-the-arroyo sequence) will be lighter, but I’d rather not understate the headcount and then have to amend the permit.

Also, I incorporated your notes on dates of use: October 14–16 for primary filming, with the 13th and 17th listed as potential weather backups. I added a generic line about you having a court-related obligation on October 11 that could require additional prep time on the 12th–13th, so we’re signaling that those days are soft holds rather than guaranteed use.

If you’re comfortable with the 28-person cap, I’ll finalize this language and send you the full packet for review this afternoon.

Thanks,
Jenna

Date: 2026-09-29 09:04
Subject: Clarification on Personnel Count – Sunridge “End of the Road”

Good morning,

I’m completing the Special Recreation Permit application for the Sunridge Outfitters “End of the Road” commercial shoot west of Albuquerque and had a quick question about how you prefer we count personnel.

Our on-site group will be relatively small and self-contained. For planning purposes, I’ve been working with a core number of **28 people** total, including production cast and crew, plus client/agency representatives and the medic. However, there’s a possibility that 1–2 additional background performers may be added late if the client decides they want a slightly fuller camping scene.

Do you prefer that I list the maximum possible headcount (i.e., 28 plus a note that we may add up to 2 more background actors), or should I only list the confirmed personnel and then email an update if we add anyone? We’ll be using the same pullouts and the dry arroyo about 0.5 miles south of the main road segment where my partner, Carlos Ramirez, rolled our Subaru Outback on October 3, 2022, during a storm; we plan to stage walk-and-talks near the washout where the tow truck from Rio Rancho Recovery pulled the car up the bank and where the sheriff’s deputy took the accident report.

Thanks in advance for the guidance – I want to make sure the form reflects your expectations.

Best,

Maria Elena Sanchez
Line Producer / On-Site Production Manager
High Desert Horizon Productions, LLC

Dial-out from home landline: (505) 268-9921 (better reception than cell in the back room).

Agenda:
- Confirm that we’re staging night shots at the same pullout on the BLM access road near Rio Puerco where my younger brother-in-law got cited by a Sandoval County deputy on March 14, 2021, for open container and expired registration while we were parked stargazing. Emphasize that we’ll stay within that already disturbed area and the adjacent 200–300 feet of roadside shoulder.
- Review any additional restrictions for evening vehicle access, generator use, and on-site safety, including communication expectations (who must be reachable by telephone at all times).
- Verify which contact number BLM wants on the permit form for day-of coordination: production office main line (505) 271-8843 vs. my personal cell.
- Ask whether they need a separate 24-hour emergency contact listed for the shoot dates and if so, whether they prefer a landline.

Notes:
Have call sheets and draft permit open to update any required phone numbers in real time during the conversation.

metadata.title: Call with BLM about night shots & access pullout

From: Maria Elena Sanchez <m.sanchez@highdeserthorizon.com>
To: Dana Cho <permits@sunridgeoutfitters.com>
Date: 2026-08-29 09:18
Subject: BLM Application – Contact & Fax Details

Hi Dana,

I’m cleaning up the BLM permit application draft you sent over and had a quick question about the “applicant information” section, specifically the fax line.

For production paperwork, I usually list the main office fax at High Desert Horizon Productions: (505) 555-9823. That’s the number our accountant uses when she sends over IRS installment agreement notices and anything related to my back self-employment taxes. Since March 2024 I’ve been on a $18,600 IRS installment plan with a $650/month payment, so I’m trying to keep all the finance-related correspondence funneled through the same fax for my records.

If BLM prefers a personal fax, the only other option I have is the shared machine at the coworking space down the hall from us: (505) 552-4410. It’s reliable but sometimes incoming pages get mixed in with other tenants’ stuff, which makes me nervous for anything time-sensitive like the dates-of-use confirmation.

Speaking of dates, remember we’ll be on BLM land October 14–16, 2026, with October 13 and 17 as contingency days. I really need to be back in Albuquerque the evening of the 15th to drive my dad Jorge home after his chemo at Presbyterian, since my mom’s not comfortable driving at night anymore after her minor stroke last year.

Let me know which fax you’d prefer I list in the applicant info so it’s consistent with Sunridge’s internal files.

Best,
Maria

Hi Maria,

Can you confirm which one you actually want there? Subject: Clarification on Contact Numbers for March Shoot Application

Hi there,

I’m double-checking the contact section before I resubmit the SF-299 for our March 10–14, 2026 shoot.

For the “applicant information” block, I listed my cell as the primary phone and our production office line as the secondary: (505) 555-8829. For the fax phone, I originally put the general High Desert Horizon fax, (505) 555-9931, but our line producer in Santa Fe is nudging me to use her machine instead since she’s the one who baby-sits incoming permit correspondence. If it’s acceptable to you, I’d like to switch the fax number to her desk fax: (505) 555-6640.

I’m also updating the location language. We’re planning to film on the same BLM parcel off County Road 279 where Carlos’s DUI checkpoint incident happened on March 18, 2023, just west of the exit for the Mesita Clinic, about 42 miles west of Albuquerque—roughly the area our attorney at Lopez & Herrera Law Firm marked in the police report photos. Lastly, on the “hours of use” line, I’d written 06:00–18:30, but I may need to note I’ll personally be on site as early as 04:30 on March 12 so I can call my dad before his 05:00 chemotherapy session, then stay late to make up prep.

Best,
Maria

From: Carlos Ramirez <c.ramirez@mesitacharterschool.org>
To: Maria Elena Sanchez <maria.sanchez@highdeserthorizon.com>
Date: 2025-03-11 21:04
Subject: Wrong Fax on the Form?

Hey Mari,

I was going through the copies you left on the kitchen table (the BLM stuff for that desert commercial) and I noticed something that might bite you later.

On the page where it asks for “applicant information,” they’ve got separate lines for phone, email, and fax phone. You wrote your cell and the office landline, but for the fax you scribbled my work fax at the school, (505) 555-3179. That’s the maintenance office machine, and half the time it’s out of paper. Please don’t have federal permits coming through there.

Did you mean to put the production fax (505) 555-9931 or the little desktop one you bought for home, the cheap Brother unit with the Nob Hill line, (505) 555-6084? If they send anything about the parcel off County Road 279 where my DUI checkpoint mess happened, you really don’t want that landing in the school office.

Also, I saw the note about you being on site at 04:30 so you can call your dad before chemo. If you want, I can handle school drop-off those days so you’re not racing back from the Rio Puerco Field Office.

Text me when you see this so I know which fax you actually want on that form.

Love,
Carlos
